stories.headline: Uber Co-Founder Travis Kalanick Reimagines Real Estate Company as Robotics Venture
stories.summary: Travis Kalanick, co-founder of Uber, has shifted his real estate company's focus to robotics technology, releasing a manifesto describing his approach of 'treating atoms like bits.' The pivot represents Kalanick's return to the technology sector with a robotics-centered business model.
stories.original_title: Co-Founder of Uber Pivots Real Estate Company to Robots, Posts Manifesto — Travis Kalanick is back, and he's "treating atoms like bits."
stories.context: Uber started as a simple idea in 2009 - let people use their phones to request rides from regular car owners instead of calling taxi companies. The company grew massively and disrupted (changed how things worked in) the entire transportation industry, but its co-founder Travis Kalanick left under pressure in 2017. After leaving Uber, Kalanick started a real estate company that was supposed to change how buildings are bought and managed. Now he's changed directions completely - instead of dealing with buildings, he wants to build robots. His new plan of "treating atoms like bits" is basically saying he wants to use computer-thinking (how we handle digital information) to control physical objects in the real world (robots that move around and do things).
stories.why_it_matters: This matters because when successful entrepreneurs start building robot companies, it usually means they see robots becoming a huge part of our daily lives soon. His experience from changing how people get around with cars could help him figure out how robots might change how we move and build things in the real world.
stories.other_side: Some people worry that Kalanick's track record includes lots of controversy and problems at Uber, so they question whether he should be trusted with building powerful robots that could eventually work everywhere.

stories.headline: Cybersecurity Threats Escalate with AWS Account Hijacking and HR-Targeted Malware Campaign
stories.summary: Security researchers uncovered an AiTM phishing kit designed to hijack AWS accounts, alongside a year-long malware campaign specifically targeting HR departments. The incidents highlight growing cybersecurity threats facing cloud infrastructure and human resource systems.
stories.original_title: Week in review: AiTM phishing kit used to hijack AWS accounts, year-long malware campaign targets HR — Here's an overview of some of last week's most interesting news, articles, interviews and videos: Turning expertise into opportunity for women in cybersecurity Speaker diversity in cybersecurity has been a talking point for over a decade, with panels, pledges…
stories.context: The modern internet runs on "cloud computing," which means instead of companies owning their own giant computer rooms, they rent computing power from big providers like Amazon Web Services (AWS). It's like renting office space instead of buying a building - it's cheaper and easier, but also means your business is at someone else's mercy. Hackers constantly try to steal access to these cloud accounts because it gives them access tons of valuable information and computing power. At the same time, HR (Human Resources) departments are particularly good targets for hackers because they handle all the sensitive employee information - Social Security numbers, bank accounts, personal details, and company secrets. Both of these attack patterns - stealing cloud access and targeting HR - represent how organized and sophisticated hackers have become at finding weak spots in how modern businesses operate.
stories.why_it_matters: These attacks are dangerous because hackers are getting better at stealing access to the computer systems that run big companies and the websites where people get hired. When these systems get hacked, it can lead to money being stolen, private information leaking, or businesses being shut down completely.
stories.other_side: Some argue that reporting every attack might scare people and companies too much, making them less willing to use helpful cloud services or online HR tools that actually make business more efficient.

stories.headline: Affordable GameMT EX8 Retro Handheld Surprises with Unexpected Quality
stories.summary: The $140 GameMT EX8 retro gaming handheld has emerged as a standout in a crowded market of similar devices, offering superior performance and design despite sharing common components with competitors. The device challenges the perception that budget retro handhelds sacrifice quality for affordability.
stories.original_title: This $140 GameMT EX8 Retro Handheld Wasn't Supposed To Be This Good — The retro gaming handheld market has a crowding problem. Every few weeks, another device shows up running the same MediaTek chip, the same emulators, and the same vaguely familiar industrial design. Most of them blur together, and that sameness has made it ha…
stories.context: Retro gaming is a huge market where people want to play old video games from systems like Nintendo, Sega, and others that don't exist anymore. In recent years, many small companies have started making handheld devices that can play old games digitally, but most of them are cheaply made and poorly designed because the manufacturers are trying to make money quickly rather than make good products. These devices usually share the same basic electronic parts and chips inside, so they all have similar performance capabilities. The GameMT EX8 is special because it shows that a company can use standard parts but still design a quality product that stands out - proving that you don't have to sacrifice quality to sell something at a lower price point. This matters because it sets a new expectation for buyers and forces other companies in this space to improve their products or lose customers.
stories.why_it_matters: This matters because it shows you don't always have to pay premium prices to get good products anymore, which could change how companies price their gadgets. It proves that smart design and good choices can beat fancy brands, pushing other companies to either lower their prices or make better stuff.
stories.other_side: Some critics say that while it looks good now, cheaper devices often break faster or don't last as long as more expensive options, so you might end up paying more in the long run anyway.

stories.headline: Grindr Reveals 70% of Code Contributions Now AI-Generated Using Claude Code
stories.summary: Grindr's Chief Product Officer AJ Balance announced that 70% of the company's code is now being checked in via AI tools, specifically Claude Code, marking a significant shift in software engineering practices. The company is also leveraging AI for advertising strategy and testing a premium $350 subscription tier.
stories.original_title: Grindr's CPO says software engineering is 'changing in a good way' and 70% of its code is being checked in via AI — Grindr's CPO, AJ Balance, told Business Insider about the company's AI and ads strategy, using Claude Code, and testing a $350 subscription tier.
stories.context: For decades, writing computer code was a manual process where humans typed out every single line of instructions for a program. This is extremely time-consuming and requires years of training and practice to do well. But now, artificial intelligence tools (like Claude Code) can write or suggest code for human programmers, similar to how autocorrect or autocomplete suggests words when you're typing. Grindr is pushing this to the extreme by letting AI handle 70% of their programming work - which means instead of a team of 10 human programmers, they might only need 3 or 4 who work alongside the AI as editors rather than original creators. This represents a fundamental shift in how software is built and could eventually change what it means to be a programmer in the modern workforce.
stories.why_it_matters: This is a big deal because if AI is writing most of the computer code now, it could completely change what human programmers do for work. It shows that artificial intelligence isn't just helping people write code anymore—it's becoming the main writer, which might spread to other industries too.
stories.other_side: Some experts worry that relying on AI to write code might hide mistakes or problems that human eyes would normally catch, potentially making apps and websites more buggy or unsafe.

stories.headline: ByteDance Suspends Launch of Video AI Model Amid Copyright Disputes
stories.summary: ByteDance, the parent company of TikTok, has paused the planned mid-March global launch of its new video AI model following copyright-related conflicts. The suspension reflects growing legal and intellectual property challenges facing AI companies developing generative media tools.
stories.original_title: ByteDance suspends launch of video AI model after copyright disputes: Report — ByteDance had been aiming ​to make the new video model available to ⁠customers worldwide in mid-March.
stories.context: Generative AI is a type of artificial intelligence that doesn't just analyze information but actually creates new things - like writing stories, making images, or creating videos. These systems are trained by feeding them massive amounts of existing content, and they learn patterns from that content to create new works that are similar to what they've seen. The problem is that training an AI often involves using copyrighted material - like movies, images, videos, and music - without paying the original creators who made that work. This has led to lawsuits and legal battles across the AI industry. When ByteDance (the Chinese company that owns TikTok) decided not to release their new video AI model because of copyright concerns, it shows how these legal disputes are starting to slow down the progress of AI development and forcing companies to be more careful about how they build their technology.
stories.why_it_matters: This pause matters because it shows that even huge tech companies can't just take whatever they want from the internet to build AI tools without permission. It might force all AI companies to start paying artists and creators for their work, or find new ways to train their systems without stealing content.
stories.other_side: Some people argue that these copyright rules are too old-fashioned and make it harder for companies to create cool new tools that could help everyone, holding back innovation in technology.

stories.headline: Ukraine Seeks Financial and Technological Support for Drone Defense Partnership With Middle East
stories.summary: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy announced that Ukraine is sharing its expertise in countering Iranian kamikaze drones with Middle Eastern nations including Qatar, UAE, Saudi Arabia, and a U.S. base in Jordan. In exchange, Ukraine is requesting financial assistance and technology transfers from its regional partners.
stories.original_title: Zelenskiy says Ukraine wants money, technology in return for Middle East drone help —
stories.context: Iran has developed "kamikaze drones," which are unmanned flying vehicles loaded with explosives that fly into targets and destroy themselves upon impact - essentially cheaper versions of missile systems. These drones became widely known during the Ukraine war, where they've been used to attack Ukrainian cities and infrastructure. Through repeated attacks Ukraine has had to learn how to detect, track, and shoot down these drones effectively, becoming quite skilled at it. These Middle Eastern countries (Qatar, UAE, Saudi Arabia) are interested in this knowledge because they face similar threats in their region. Meanwhile, Ukraine desperately needs money and advanced military technology to continue defending itself, so they're trading their hard-earned expertise for resources - it's a simple mutually beneficial deal where both sides get something they need.
stories.why_it_matters: This partnership is important because Ukraine's hard-earned knowledge from defending against real drone attacks can help protect oil-rich countries that are also targets. More importantly, the money and technology Ukraine gets back could help them keep fighting in their own war when other support might be running low.
stories.other_side: Some critics worry that sharing military technology could end up arming dangerous people or backfire in the future, and that this deal is less about defense and more about getting cash any way possible.

stories.headline: Ukraine Exchanges Drone Defense Expertise for Technology Funding With Gulf Nations
stories.summary: Ukraine has deployed specialists to Qatar, the United Arab Emirates, Saudi Arabia, and a U.S. military base in Jordan to share knowledge on intercepting Iranian-made kamikaze drones. The strategic partnership aims to secure both financial resources and technological assistance for Ukraine's defense capabilities.
stories.original_title: Zelenskiy says Ukraine wants money, technology in return for Middle East drone help — Ukraine is sharing its expertise in downing Iranian kamikaze drones with four Middle Eastern countries. Kyiv has sent specialists to Qatar, the United Arab Emirates, Saudi Arabia, and a US military base in Jordan. Ukraine seeks financial and technological ass…
stories.context: The war in Ukraine, which began when Russia invaded in February 2022, has become the testing ground for modern warfare technology. Ukraine has had to defend against Russian attacks using relatively inexpensive Iranian-made drones that destroy themselves when hitting targets - a new and challenging type of threat that traditional military systems weren't built to handle. Through necessity, Ukrainian forces have developed effective ways to counter these drones, turning vulnerability into expertise. The countries in the Middle East mentioned are wealthy nations with modern militaries that worry about facing similar drone attacks in their region. By sending Ukrainian specialists to share what they've learned about defending against these particular types of drones, Ukraine is essentially selling military expertise and experience in exchange for funding and technology transfers - creating a partnership that benefits both sides without requiring a direct political alliance.
stories.why_it_matters: This matters because Ukraine is showing how a country under attack can turn its painful war experience into valuable expertise that other countries will pay for. It creates a new way for Ukraine to get money and weapons when regular help from other countries might be slowing down, while making the Middle East safer at the same time.
stories.other_side: Some people fear that trading military secrets for money could eventually teach future enemies how to defend against Western weapons, or that the technology could end up in the wrong hands through these partner countries.
